question_title: Will the U.S. Supreme Court’s current Granted & Noted List show at least five distinct cases with “Granted” dates from March 5, 2026 through April 30, 2026 ?
summary: The question asks whether the OT 2025 Granted & Noted List (25grantednotedlist.pdf) will show 5+ cases with "Granted" dates from March 5-April 30, 2026. My research reveals a structural feature that makes this nearly impossible: this list only contains cases scheduled for argument during OT 2025, and cases granted in spring 2026 go on the OT 2026 list instead. I verified this directly - the March 9, 2026 cert grant (Dept. of Air Force v. Guahan) appears on the 26grantednotedlist.pdf, not the 25grantednotedlist.pdf. The Court has essentially finished selecting OT 2025 cases (last cert grant on OT 2025 list: 1/16/26). For YES, 5+ cases would need to be granted AND fast-tracked for current-term argument — unprecedented in modern history. Historical base rate is 0 cases per term.

factors.description: March-April 2026 cert grants go on the OT 2026 list, not the OT 2025 list. Confirmed by checking both PDFs: the March 9 grant (Dept. of Air Force v. Guahan) appears on 26grantednotedlist.pdf, not 25grantednotedlist.pdf.

The Court has essentially finished selecting cases for OT 2025 arguments. SCOTUSblog reported in January 2026 that the last case for this term's docket was granted 1/26/26. Latest grant on OT 2025 list is 1/16/26.
Historical base rate of 0: In OT 2024, zero cert grants with March-April dates appeared on the OT 2024 list. The latest OT 2024 cert grant was 1/27/25.

Emergency/expedited cases are theoretically possible but extremely rare. Even in politically turbulent times (Trump admin), the Court typically fast-tracks 0-1 cert grants per term, not 5. Stay applications don't count per fine print.

The Trump administration has been generating an unusual number of emergency SCOTUS cases, slightly increasing the chance of expedited grants. But stay applications are excluded, and even 5 expedited cert grants would be unprecedented.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (YES)**

The key finding driving this low estimate is structural: the OT 2025 Granted & Noted List (25grantednotedlist.pdf) only contains cases scheduled for argument during OT 2025. Cases granted in March-April 2026 are placed on the OT 2026 list instead. I verified this directly:

- **OT 2026 list** (26grantednotedlist.pdf, as of March 9, 2026) already contains the March 9 cert grant (Dept. of Air Force v. Guahan) — NOT on the OT 2025 list
- **OT 2025 list** (25grantednotedlist.pdf, as of March 4, 2026) has no grants after 1/16/26
- SCOTUSblog reported in January 2026 that the Court has "probably chosen all the cases it will hear this term"
- **Historical base rate**: Zero cert grants with March-April dates appeared on the analogous OT 2024 list (latest was 1/27/25)

For YES to happen, 5+ cases would need to be granted cert AND fast-tracked for remaining OT 2025 arguments (through late April) — all within roughly 8 weeks. This is historically unprecedented. Even emergency stay applications (like Trump v. CASA) are excluded by the fine print since they aren't cert grants.
sources_consulted: [List of pending United States Supreme Court cases - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_pending_United_States_Supreme_Court_cases)
[The Supreme Court has (probably) chosen all the cases it will hear this term](https://www.scotusblog.com/2026/01/the-supreme-court-has-probably-chosen-all-the-cases-it-will-hear-this-term/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the Supreme Court’s Granted & Noted List for October Term 2025 at https://www.supremecourt.gov/orders/25grantednotedlist.pdf shows at least five distinct cases with “Granted” dates from March 5, 2026 through April 30, 2026 inclusive before April 30, 2026. Otherwise, it resolves to no.
fine_print: Count each case once. Count only lines marked with a "Granted" date in the stated range. Do not count cases merely "Noted," emergency applications, original-jurisdiction matters, reinstatements, or other procedural entries unless the list expressly shows a certiorari grant for a distinct case in the date range. Candidate Sources: Granted/Noted Cases List landing page at https://www.supremecourt.gov/grantednotedlist/24grantednotedlist ; the current orders page at https://www.supremecourt.gov/orders/ordersofthecourt/25.
